text: Sluggard. Who neglects his own and others' welfare. (Calmet)<hr>Disgrace. He is still despised here, and punished eternally, Matthew 25:30. (Worthington)
Pelted. Greek, "compared to a lump of dung." (Haydock)
Loss. "I wish," said Augustus, "I had never married, or that I had died without children." (Suetonius)
Inheritance. Her wisdom and economy, Proverbs 31:29. Women had no inheritance among the Jews, when they had brothers.
Bold. In speaking and acting without restraint. (Calmet)

Mourning. St. Jerome (ep. ad Julian) quoting this sentence, calls it "divine Scripture," Proverb 25:20.<hr>Of time. Or "stripes and instruction well-timed, are wisdom." (Haydock)<hr>Only the wise know how to correct with advantage.
Fool. Who is incapable of understanding. Those who have sense may be corrected, though they may have taken evil courses.
This? The Athenian philosophers and Festus derided St. Paul, Acts 17:32.

For the fool. In the language of the Holy Ghost, he is styled a fool that turns away from God to follow vanity and sin. And what is said by the wise man against fools is meant of such fools as these. (Challoner)<hr>Their wicked life is worse than death. (Worthington)

Days. There was a longer time assigned for great men, Genesis 50:3., Numbers 20:30., and Deuteronomy 34:8.<hr>Life. This is understood of those who have lost all reason, or sense of religion. St. Monica wept for her son, hoping that he would repent, as a certain bishop had comforted her. (St. Augustine, Confessions 3:ultra.[last chapter])
Lead? Gold alone is heavier. But the fool is compared with lead, and is most insupportable.
Wood. Such were used in the walls of Jerusalem, (4 Machabees ii.) and of the temple and palace, 3 Kings 6:36., and 7:12.
Cost. Of lime. (Calmet)<hr>Greek omits this comparison, as well as the 23d verse. (Haydock)
Not fear. This seems contrary to what goes before. But fools are sometimes intrepid even to harshness. (Calmet)
Resentment. Or "sense," (Haydock) by means of pungent reprimands. (Calmet)

Away. Greek literally, "will knock them down," dejiciet. (Haydock)<hr>Deliberate provocations (ver. 27.) are pardoned with more difficulty than a sudden impulse of wrath, ver. 26. On such occasions we may withdraw our confidence, but not our charity and patience, Matthew 5:11. (Calmet)<hr>A true friend will not be lost for a temporal damage; but he must not be slighted. (Worthington)
Inheritance. Greek adds, "for poverty is not always contemptible, nor is the senseless rich worthy of admiration." (Haydock)
Of him. Yet I will not follow their example. (Calmet)<hr>If my friend has behaved ill to me, he will be the greatest sufferer. (Haydock)
Not. Psalm 140:3. Who can sufficiently guard his tongue? (Calmet)
